She kept returning, kept arguing her case, and kept making dua to Allah. According to some narrations, revelation came while she was still present. According to others, she left and returned multiple times.

Aisha’s Testimony

Sayyidah Aisha (RA) narrated that she was in the same room when Khawlah came to argue her case. Aisha said: “Blessed is the One whose hearing encompasses all things. I was in the room and I could hear some of her speech but some of it was hidden from me, yet Allah heard all of it from above seven heavens.” This testimony emphasizes that even though Aisha was physically present, she couldn’t catch every word - but Allah heard everything perfectly.

Stages of Legislation

This surah represents an important stage in Islamic legislation regarding marital matters:

  1. Pre-Islamic: Zihar was a form of divorce that left women in legal limbo
  2. Early Islam: The practice initially remained, with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم advising separation based on the zihar statement
  3. Revelation of Surah Al-Mujadilah: Allah revealed that zihar is not divorce but a sinful statement that requires expiation (kaffarah)

The kaffarah has three levels: - First: Free a believing slave - If unable: Fast two consecutive months - If unable: Feed sixty poor people

This is the same three-level kaffarah mentioned for other serious violations, showing the gravity of zihar.

Comparison to Biblical Law

While the Bible doesn’t specifically address zihar (as it was an Arab custom), there are relevant parallels regarding divorce and treatment of wives:

  1. Deuteronomy 24:1-4: Discusses divorce procedures and prohibits a man from remarrying a wife he divorced if she married someone else in between. Islamic law has similar provisions.

  2. Malachi 2:16: “For I hate divorce, says the Lord” - showing that God’s preference across scriptures is for marriage preservation, which is why Allah made zihar difficult to maintain (requiring kaffarah) rather than making it an easy form of divorce.

  3. 1 Corinthians 7:10-11: Advises married couples not to separate, and if they do separate, to remain unmarried or reconcile. This shows the Judeo-Christian tradition also valued marital stability.

The Islamic solution to zihar is unique in that it: - Doesn’t make it a valid divorce - Requires the husband to pay a penalty if he wants to return to his wife - Gives the wife options rather than leaving her suspended - Addresses the underlying injustice of the pre-Islamic practice

The Broader Historical Context: Treatment of Women

This incident occurred during the Madinan period when Islamic law was being fully developed. Several surahs and ayahs were revealed addressing women’s rights:

  • Surah An-Nisa (4): Revealed earlier, addressing marriage, divorce, inheritance
  • Surah Al-Ahzab (33): Addressing proper interaction with the Prophet’s wives and believing women
  • Surah At-Talaq (65): Revealed later, giving detailed divorce procedures
  • Surah Al-Mumtahanah (60): Addressing women who emigrate

The revelation of Surah Al-Mujadilah fits into this broader framework of establishing women’s rights and proper marital conduct in Islam. It shows Allah’s attention to even one woman’s complaint and His willingness to legislate in response to injustice.

The Incident of the Man Who Broke His Fast

The lecture references another historical incident (mentioned in Sahih Bukhari and Muslim) where a man came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم saying he had been intimate with his wife during Ramadan, breaking his fast. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم applied the same three-level kaffarah:

  1. Free a slave - “I can’t”
  2. Fast two consecutive months - “I can’t”
  3. Feed sixty people - “I can’t”

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم then received some dates and gave them to the man to distribute as charity. When the man said there was no one poorer than his family between the two mountains (of Madinah), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم smiled and told him to feed his own family.

This incident shows: 1. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم applied the kaffarah of zihar to a different but related sin (violating the sanctity of Ramadan through marital intimacy) 2. The goal of Islamic law is mercy and finding solutions, not punishment 3. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم was performing qiyas (analogical reasoning) - applying a revealed law to a similar unrevealed situation

No Shyness in Seeking Knowledge

Both the incident of Khawlah and the man who broke his fast show that the companions (RA) had no shame in seeking guidance about intimate marital matters. This is a principle in Islam - there is no shyness in matters of religion. As Sayyidah Aisha (RA) said: “How excellent are the women of the Ansar; shyness did not prevent them from seeking knowledge about their religion.”

Coherence

Connection to Previous Revelation

Surah Al-Mujadilah (58) is preceded by Surah Al-Hadid (57), which ends with powerful reminders about Allah’s knowledge and control over all affairs. The transition from Al-Hadid to Al-Mujadilah is thematically coherent:

  • Al-Hadid ends discussing those who were given the Book before (Jews and Christians) and how some hearts became hardened
  • Al-Mujadilah begins with Allah’s perfect hearing and seeing - qualities that distinguish Him from the false gods and establish His ability to respond to human needs

The surah name itself - “The Woman Who Disputes” or “The Pleading Woman” - is taken from this very first ayah, showing its centrality to the surah’s message.

Internal Coherence Within Surah Al-Mujadilah

The surah proceeds with remarkable coherence:

  1. Ayah 1: Establishes that Allah hears and sees - the foundation for justice
  2. Ayahs 2-4: Provide the ruling on zihar and its expiation
  3. Ayahs 5-6: Warn those who oppose Allah and His Messenger
  4. Ayahs 7-10: Discuss secret conversations and proper conduct
  5. Ayahs 11-13: Address proper etiquette in gatherings with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم
  6. Ayahs 14-22: Condemn hypocrites and those who befriend enemies of Allah

The first ayah sets the stage by establishing Allah’s perfect awareness. This is crucial for the legal rulings that follow - only a Lord who truly hears and sees can legislate justly on such intimate matters.

Connection to Surah At-Tahrim (66)

The lecture references Surah At-Tahrim, which also deals with marital issues involving the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم:

  • At-Tahrim begins: “O Prophet, why do you prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking to please your wives?”
  • This incident involved the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم making something halal (likely honey or being with Maria the Copt) haram upon himself due to pressure from some wives
  • Allah revealed that this was not appropriate - you cannot make haram what Allah made halal

The parallel to zihar is clear: just as a husband cannot make halal (his wife) into haram (by calling her like his mother), the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم could not make halal things haram. Both incidents teach that only Allah determines what is lawful and unlawful.

Connection to Ayahs About Proper Address of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم

The lecture references ayahs that prohibit addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم in the casual way one addresses others:

  • Surah Al-Hujurat (49:2-3): “Do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet, and do not speak loudly to him as you speak loudly to one another, lest your deeds become worthless while you do not perceive…”
  • These ayahs establish that speaking to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم requires adab (proper manners)

Yet in this incident, the woman is described as engaged in جِدَال (jadal - argumentation/debate) with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, and Allah does not criticize this. The coherence lies in understanding context: - Casual, disrespectful address is prohibited - Seeking justice, even if it requires presenting arguments, is not only permitted but honored - The difference is in intention and manner: she was arguing her case respectfully but firmly, not disrespecting the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم

Connection to the Theme of Marital Rights Throughout the Quran

This ayah connects to the broader Quranic emphasis on marital rights:

  1. Surah An-Nisa (4:34): “Men are protectors and maintainers of women” - with rights come responsibilities
  2. Surah An-Nisa (4:19): “Live with them in kindness” - the general principle
  3. Surah Al-Baqarah (2:228): “Women have rights similar to those over them in kindness” - mutual rights
  4. Surah At-Talaq (65:1): Proper divorce procedures - if separation must happen, it should be just
  5. Surah Al-Baqarah (2:231): Do not harm women through divorce procedures

Zihar violated these principles by: - Treating the wife unjustly (not living in kindness) - Suspending her in limbo (denying her rights) - Using sacred relationships (mother) in vain

The revelation in Surah Al-Mujadilah restores coherence to Islamic marital law by addressing this injustice.

Connection to “Mithaq Ghaliz” (Solemn Covenant)

The lecture references Surah An-Nisa (4:21): “How can you take it back after you have already enjoyed each other intimately and they (your wives) have taken from you a solemn covenant (mithaqan ghaliza)?”

The term مِيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا (mithaqan ghaliza) - “solemn/firm covenant” - is used only twice in the Quran: 1. For the covenant Allah took from the Prophets (33:7) 2. For the marriage covenant

This creates powerful coherence: marriage is not a casual contract but a sacred covenant comparable to the covenant of prophethood. Therefore, violating it through zihar (or any abuse) is a serious matter requiring serious expiation.

Connection to the Concept of Seeking Help

The lecture draws connections to other Quranic narratives where people had nowhere to turn except Allah:

  1. Ya’qub (AS) and Yusuf (AS): “I only complain of my suffering and grief to Allah” (12:86)
  2. Musa’s mother: Trusting Allah when placing Musa in the basket (28:7)
  3. The believers at Badr: Outnumbered and turning to Allah (8:9)

The coherence is that Allah responds to those who turn to Him in desperation. This ayah (58:1) establishes that pattern: Khawlah turned to Allah in her complaint, and Allah responded with legislation.

Connection to Justice and Legal Process

This ayah connects to the broader Quranic theme of justice:

  • The woman didn’t seek vigilante justice
  • She didn’t just complain to everyone
  • She went to the proper authority (the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم)
  • When he couldn’t immediately help, she turned to Allah
  • Allah responded through revelation

This establishes the proper channels for seeking justice in Islam, creating coherence with other legal passages in the Quran.

Connection to Following Ayahs

The first ayah’s emphasis on Allah hearing and seeing creates perfect coherence with what follows:

  • Ayah 2: “Those who pronounce zihar…” - Now that we know Allah hears and sees, here’s His ruling
  • Ayah 3: The kaffarah required - freeing a slave, fasting, feeding
  • Ayah 4: The wisdom behind this - so you may believe in Allah and His Messenger

The structure is: Divine awareness (ayah 1) → Divine legislation (ayahs 2-3) → Divine wisdom (ayah 4).

Psychological, Political & Social

Psychological: The Mind of the Oppressed

This ayah reveals psychological insights about those who suffer injustice:

The Need to Be Heard: The woman’s persistence in coming to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم repeatedly shows a fundamental human need - to be heard and validated. Even before a solution comes, the oppressed need someone to listen and acknowledge their pain. Allah emphasizes that He heard her, validating this psychological need.

The Courage to Speak Up: Psychologically, victims of domestic issues often suffer in silence due to: - Shame (“what will people think?”) - Fear (“he might get angrier”) - Hopelessness (“nothing will change”) - Pressure to maintain appearances (“keep family matters private”)

Khawlah’s courage to speak up, to argue her case publicly, to refuse an unjust answer - this is psychologically empowering. Allah immortalized her action in Quran, teaching victims that seeking help is not shameful but honorable.

The Psychology of Abuse Through Language: The lecture emphasizes how words can be used as tools of control and abuse. Zihar is psychological abuse - the husband says words that trap the wife in limbo. She’s not divorced (can’t remarry) but not fully married (can’t have normal relations). This creates psychological torture. Modern parallels include: - Threatening divorce repeatedly without following through - Using degrading language - Making the spouse feel worthless - Creating constant instability

Allah’s response shows He takes psychological abuse as seriously as physical harm.

The Psychology of Turning to Allah: When all human help fails, turning to Allah isn’t just spiritual - it’s psychologically therapeutic. Research shows that faith and prayer help people cope with stress and trauma. Khawlah’s turning to Allah (تَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللَّهِ) is presented as a model - when you have nowhere else to turn, you always have Allah.

Social Dynamics: Gender Roles and Power

This ayah addresses critical social dynamics:

Power Imbalance in Marriage: The pre-Islamic practice of zihar reveals how language can be weaponized by those with power. Men had the power to pronounce zihar and trap women in legal limbo. This represents systemic power imbalance. Allah’s legislation equalizes this by: - Making zihar not a valid divorce - Requiring expensive kaffarah to maintain it - Giving women the right to complain and seek justice

The Right to Argue: The word تُجَادِلُكَ (tujadiluka - “argues with you”) is socially significant. In many cultures, women are expected to be silent and accepting. But Allah describes this woman arguing with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم Himself and doesn’t criticize her for it. This establishes that: - Women have the right to present their case - Argumentation (in proper context) is not disrespect - Seeking justice requires active engagement, not passive acceptance

Public vs. Private Matters: The lecture addresses a critical social tension: when should family matters remain private, and when should they be made public? The teaching here is: - Seek help from qualified authorities - Don’t broadcast to everyone - But don’t suffer in silence due to shame - The right to privacy doesn’t mean covering up injustice

The Role of Community Leadership: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم serves as the model for how leaders should respond to complaints from community members, especially women: - Listen patiently - Don’t dismiss concerns - Give them time to fully explain - Don’t shame them for coming forward - If you don’t know the answer, admit it - Seek divine guidance

This creates a model for imams, counselors and community leaders.

Social Dynamics: The Extended Family

The lecture mentions that when the issue was brought to Abu Bakr (RA) as a potential mediator between his daughter Aisha and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم, he became upset at Aisha’s tone. This reveals:

Family Loyalty vs. Justice: Abu Bakr (RA) was caught between being Aisha’s father and being the Prophet’s companion. His initial reaction was to defend the Prophet’s honor. But the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم stopped him, showing that: - Even family members should let the aggrieved speak - Silencing someone, even with good intentions, isn’t always appropriate - Justice requires hearing all sides

Generational Differences: The lecture describes how some people’s inability to control their tongue comes from what they witnessed growing up. This addresses: - Intergenerational trauma - Learned behaviors in marriage - The cycle of abuse - How childhood experiences shape marital conduct

This is a call for psychological awareness - not all marital problems are purely spiritual; some have psychological roots requiring therapeutic intervention.

Political Implications: Divine Legislation

Allah as the Ultimate Legislator: The ayah establishes that Allah hears individual complaints and legislates in response. This has political implications: - No human authority is above divine law - Legislation should respond to real human suffering - Laws that trap people unjustly must be reformed - The voice of even one oppressed person matters

The Role of Revelation in Governance: This incident shows revelation wasn’t abstract theology but practical legislation addressing real social problems. This establishes that: - Islamic law is responsive, not static - Divine guidance addresses actual lived experiences - The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم didn’t legislate independently but waited for revelation - Proper Islamic governance requires divine guidance

Social Psychology: Communication in Marriage

The lecture extensively discusses communication:

The Power of Words: Words have immediate consequences for men in Islamic law (divorce, zihar) because: - Men typically have more power in marriage - Their words have immediate legal effect - This responsibility should make them cautious - Women’s words have fewer immediate worldly consequences but still have accountability in the Hereafter

Non-Verbal Communication: The ayah ends with “All-Seeing” alongside “All-Hearing” because: - Body language communicates as much as words - Facial expressions convey emotion - Tears speak volumes - Physical distance or closeness means something - Eye contact or its absence communicates

This teaches that healthy communication requires awareness of both verbal and non-verbal cues.

The Psychology of Anger: The lecture mentions that some argue divorce pronounced in anger shouldn’t count. The Islamic legal position (as presented) is: - Anger isn’t an excuse for men (they must control their tongue) - Extreme emotional states aren’t excuses (the fu’ad - burning heart - is still accountable) - This teaches emotional regulation as a religious obligation

This is psychologically sophisticated - it doesn’t accept “I couldn’t control myself” as an excuse but demands emotional maturity.

Social Issue: When Women Are Heard

The lecture emphasizes that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم said most people in Hell for the sin of the tongue are women, but he balances this by noting:

Different Standards, Same Destination: While men have immediate worldly consequences and women have primarily Hereafter consequences: - Both are accountable - Neither gets a free pass - The difference is in when accountability comes - Men should fear worldly consequences - Women should fear divine consequences

This creates a balanced social dynamic where both genders must guard their tongues for different but equally compelling reasons.

The Principle of Seeking Professional Help: The lecture strongly advocates for professional counseling, which addresses social stigma:

Destigmatizing Therapy: The argument made is: - If the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم sought mediation, who are we to be above it? - Mental health and counseling shouldn’t be stigmatized - Admitting you need help is strength, not weakness - The community should normalize seeking help

This is socially progressive and psychologically sound, challenging cultural norms that equate seeking help with weakness or shame.

The Social Impact of Silence

The lecture discusses “rafila” (رفيلة) - women who keep matters inside the fort (home). This addresses: - The ethics of privacy in marriage - When privacy becomes covering for abuse - The difference between protective privacy and harmful silence - How to balance keeping family matters private while seeking appropriate help

The model established is: Don’t broadcast to everyone, but do seek qualified help in private.

Quranic Wisdom

The Wisdom of Divine Hearing and Seeing

The opening of this ayah - قَدْ سَمِعَ اللَّهُ (Allah has indeed heard) - contains deep wisdom about the nature of Allah and His relationship with His creation:

Allah Heard Before She Spoke: The particle قَدْ (qad) indicates Allah had already heard - not just this conversation, but everything that led to it. From the day she married, through years of her marriage, through the build-up to this crisis, through every private moment of suffering - Allah heard it all. This teaches: - Allah’s knowledge precedes our awareness - Nothing happens to us that Allah doesn’t know about - The solution was already prepared before she knew she needed it - Allah’s mercy anticipates our needs

The Wisdom of the Test: Allah could have prevented the zihar from happening. He could have struck the husband’s tongue silent. He could have revealed this law before any incident occurred. But He allowed this to happen because: - Khawlah needed to demonstrate her faith through her persistence - The ummah needed to see how to seek justice - Future generations needed this example - Laws are best understood when they address real situations - Her reward for patience is eternal

Sometimes Allah allows difficulty not because He doesn’t care, but because He wants to honor us through how we respond to that difficulty.

The Wisdom of Complementary Attributes

The ayah ends with إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ (Indeed Allah is All-Hearing, All-Seeing). These two attributes together teach:

Complete Divine Awareness: Allah doesn’t just hear words - He sees context. He doesn’t just see actions - He hears intentions. Together, these attributes mean Allah has complete awareness of our situation. This gives: - Hope to the oppressed: Allah knows your situation completely - Warning to the oppressor: You cannot hide anything from Allah - Comfort to the suffering: Your silent pain is not invisible - Accountability to all: Both our words and actions are recorded

The Wisdom of Patience: Sometimes we cry out to Allah and wonder why He doesn’t respond immediately. This ayah teaches that: - Allah hears from the very beginning - Allah’s hearing doesn’t mean immediate intervention - Sometimes the solution requires time and process - Allah’s hearing is preparation for His response - The delay is not neglect but wisdom

Wisdom About the Nature of Marriage

This incident teaches deep truths about marriage:

Marriage as Mithaq Ghaliz: The lecture connects this to the ayah calling marriage a “solemn covenant” (مِيثَاقًا غَلِيظًا). This teaches: - Marriage isn’t a casual relationship - It’s not just a civil contract - It’s a sacred bond witnessed by Allah - Breaking it or abusing it is breaking a covenant with Allah Himself - The same term used for prophetic covenant shows the gravity

The Purpose of Marriage: The lecture references that marriage’s first purpose is سَكَن (sakina - tranquility/peace), then مَوَدَّة (mawadda - love), then رَحْمَة (rahma - mercy). This wisdom teaches: - If there’s no peace, love cannot survive - Love fluctuates, but peace should be constant - Mercy comes when both parties care for each other’s well-being - Marriage without peace becomes a prison - Zihar destroyed peace, making the marriage invalid in spirit even if technically still existing

The Wisdom of Gendered Legislation

The lecture addresses a controversial but profound wisdom:

Why Men’s Words Have Immediate Consequences: Allah made men’s speech in certain contexts (divorce, zihar) have immediate worldly consequences because: - Men generally have more physical and financial power in marriage - This creates a check on that power - It forces men to think before they speak - It protects women from verbal abuse being weaponized - It teaches emotional regulation as a religious duty

This isn’t inequality - it’s equity. Different responsibilities for different roles, all aimed at justice.

Why Women’s Words Have Fewer Immediate Worldly Consequences: The wisdom is: - Women are generally in a more vulnerable position - Emotional outbursts under pressure are given more latitude - This doesn’t mean no accountability - it means accountability in the Hereafter - This prevents women from being punished twice: once by being oppressed, again by having their reaction used against them - But the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم warned women not to abuse this latitude

The Wisdom of the Kaffarah System

The three-level expiation (free slave, fast 60 days, feed 60 people) teaches:

Redemption is Possible: No matter how serious the sin, there’s a way back. This teaches: - Allah’s mercy is greater than His anger - Islam doesn’t trap people in their mistakes - But serious sins require serious expiation - The kaffarah is both punishment and purification

The Social Dimension: Each level of kaffarah has social benefit: - Freeing a slave: Liberates a human being - Fasting: Personal purification and empathy with the hungry - Feeding poor: Direct social benefit to the needy

This teaches that Islamic law considers both personal purification and social welfare.

The Wisdom of Seeking Help

The ayah validates seeking help, teaching:

There’s No Shame in Admitting You Need Help: The lecture emphasizes that if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم himself sought mediation and counsel, who are we to be above it? This teaches: - Pride that prevents seeking help is foolishness, not dignity - Intelligence is knowing when you need expertise beyond your own - Community is meant to support each other - Professional help (counseling, therapy) is not un-Islamic - The goal is solutions, not saving face

Seek the Right Help from the Right People: Khawlah didn’t complain to everyone - she went to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم. This teaches: - Not all help is good help - Qualified help is essential - Broadcasting problems to everyone makes them worse - Wise confidants who can actually help are what’s needed - Sometimes that means professional counselors, therapists, qualified scholars

The Wisdom of Balancing Fear and Hope

The lecture beautifully explains that when the man came confessing his sin, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم laughed. This teaches:

Meet Fear with Hope: When someone comes already in a state of fear and regret: - Don’t pile on more fear - Balance the equation with hope - Their coming is proof of their faith - Now they need encouragement, not further condemnation - This is the wisdom of دعوة (dawah) - knowing when to emphasize what

The Door to Forgiveness is Open: Until death, Allah accepts repentance. This teaches: - Never despair of Allah’s mercy - No sin is too great for Allah to forgive - But the time to repent is now, before death - In this life: Everything is written in pencil (can be erased with tawbah) - After death: Everything is embroidered (permanent record)

This metaphor of pencil vs. embroidery is powerful - it gives hope while emphasizing urgency.

The Wisdom of Equal Footing in Conversation

The word تَحَاوُر (tahawur - mutual conversation) teaches:

Respect in Seeking Justice: When someone seeks help: - Don’t make them feel inferior - Give them space to fully explain - Listen actively and attentively - Respond thoughtfully - Don’t dismiss their concerns - Treat them with dignity

This is the prophetic model for counseling, leadership and helping others.

The Wisdom for Women and Men

The lecture addresses both genders with profound wisdom:

For Men - Guard Your Tongue: - Your words have power and immediate effect - You will be held accountable in this life and the next - Emotional states are not excuses - The fu’ad (burning heart) is still accountable - Fear Allah in what you say to your wife - Don’t weaponize divorce - Don’t use degrading speech

For Women - Don’t Abuse the Mercy: - Just because there are fewer worldly penalties doesn’t mean no accountability - The Hereafter’s accountability is even more serious - The tongue can lead to Hell - Guard your speech even when emotional - Don’t manipulate through emotional outbursts - Don’t broadcast family matters to everyone

For Both - Communication is Everything: - Words matter (hearing) - Body language matters (seeing) - Tone matters - Timing matters - Manner matters - Intention matters

Healthy marriage requires mastery of all forms of communication.

The Ultimate Wisdom: Allah Responds to the Oppressed

The ayah’s greatest wisdom is this: Allah heard one woman’s complaint and revealed legislation in response. This teaches: - No one is too small or insignificant for Allah’s attention - Allah takes oppression seriously - Even one person’s suffering matters - Allah will make a way out for those who turn to Him - The dua of the oppressed is answered - Justice will come, even if delayed - Trust in Allah’s perfect timing and wisdom

This is the ultimate comfort for anyone facing injustice: Allah heard Khawlah, and He hears you.

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 3

يُظَاهِرُونَ (yuzahiruna) [ظ-ه-ر]

The root ظ-ه-ر relates to back, appearance, or making something manifest. The verb form used here (Form III) carries the meaning of making something public or declaring something openly. In the context of zihar, it refers to the act of declaring one’s wife to be like one’s mother’s back - an ancient pre-Islamic practice. The use of this specific form emphasizes the deliberate nature of the declaration and its public dimension, even when said privately.

يَعُودُونَ (ya’uduna) [ع-و-د]

The root ع-و-د means to return, go back, or revert. The expression يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا (ya’uduna lima qalu) literally means “they return to what they said.” This creates an interesting grammatical situation because the preposition لِ (li) can mean “to” or “because of,” creating ambiguity. Some scholars interpret this as returning FROM what they said (wanting to undo it), while others see it as returning TO the original intent (wanting to restore the relationship). The word ’awda is also used in Surah Al-Isra (17:8) where Allah says to the Israelites عُدْتُمْ عُدْنَا (’udtum ’udna) - “if you return, We return” - showing the dual meaning of return to good or return to sin.

رَقَبَةٍ (raqabatin) [ر-ق-ب]

The root ر-ق-ب relates to the neck, watching, or observing. The word رَقَبَة (raqaba) literally means neck, but idiomatically refers to a person in slavery. This connects to Allah’s name الرَّقِيب (Ar-Raqib), the Watchful One who observes from above, as a shepherd watches over sheep by their necks. Freeing a رَقَبَة means liberating someone from the collar of slavery - removing the physical and metaphorical chains from their neck. The imagery evokes the shepherd who grabs sheep by the neck to protect them from danger.

يَتَمَاسَّا (yatamassa) [م-س-س]

The root م-س-س means to touch or make contact. The Form VI verb يَتَمَاسَّا creates a reciprocal meaning - mutual touching or contact between two parties. This is one of the Quran’s euphemistic expressions for marital intimacy. Allah uses dignified language rather than explicit terms, teaching believers that even when discussing sensitive matters, one should employ respectful and indirect terminology. This same root appears throughout the Quran in various contexts related to physical contact.

تُوعَظُونَ (tu’azuna) [و-ع-ظ]

The root و-ع-ظ means to counsel, admonish, or give advice that penetrates the heart. The word مَوْعِظَة (maw’iza) refers to counsel that emotionally affects the listener, not merely intellectual instruction. Allah uses this term rather than أَمْر (command) to emphasize that this ruling should be contemplated deeply and affect one’s heart. The counsel should make one reflect on the gravity of their words and actions.

خَبِيرٌ (khabirun) [خ-ب-ر]

The root خ-ب-ر relates to news, information, and experience. The word خَبِير (khabir) means one who has intimate knowledge through direct experience, not secondhand information. When Allah describes Himself as خَبِير, He indicates comprehensive awareness of one’s complete experience and context - every circumstance, every emotion, every detail. This differs from merely hearing about something; it means Allah has full experiential knowledge of what you are going through as you experience it.

Ayah 4

يَجِدْ (yajid) [و-ج-د]

The root و-ج-د means to find or discover. The phrase فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ (faman lam yajid) - “whoever does not find” - refers to someone who cannot find the means to free a slave, whether because slaves are unavailable or because they lack the financial resources to purchase and free one.

صِيَامُ (siyamu) [ص-و-م]

The root ص-و-م means to abstain or refrain, particularly from food, drink, and other physical desires. The word صِيَام (siyam) refers to the act of fasting. In this context, it serves as an alternative expiation when freeing a slave is not possible.

شَهْرَيْنِ (shahrayni) [ش-ه-ر]

The root ش-ه-ر relates to the moon and months. شَهْرَيْنِ (shahrayn) is the dual form meaning “two months.” The specification of two consecutive months creates a sustained period of discipline and spiritual cleansing.

مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ (mutatabiayn) [ت-ب-ع]

The root ت-ب-ع means to follow or succeed. The word مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ (mutatabiayn) means consecutive or successive, emphasizing that the two months of fasting must be continuous without interruption. If the fast is broken, the count resets, ensuring the full spiritual discipline is maintained.

يَسْتَطِعْ (yastati’) [ط-و-ع]

The root ط-و-ع relates to ability, capacity, and voluntary action. The word يَسْتَطِعْ (yastati’) means “has the ability” or “is capable.” This acknowledges that some may be physically unable to fast for 60 consecutive days due to illness, age, or other legitimate reasons.

إِطْعَامُ (it’amu) [ط-ع-م]

The root ط-ع-م relates to food and feeding. The word إِطْعَامُ (it’am) means the act of feeding. This third option - feeding 60 poor people - provides an alternative for those who cannot fast, connecting back to the established principle in Quranic legislation about making up for missed fasts through feeding others.

مِسْكِينًا (miskinan) [س-ك-ن]

The root س-ك-ن relates to stillness, dwelling, and poverty. A مِسْكِين (miskin) is someone who lacks basic resources and cannot help themselves - someone in a state of need and vulnerability. The plural form implies 60 such individuals must be fed.

تُؤْمِنُوا (tu’minu) [أ-م-ن]

The root أ-م-ن relates to safety, security, and faith. The word تُؤْمِنُوا (tu’minu) means “you believe.” Allah states that following these commands demonstrates and strengthens genuine faith. This connects actions with faith, making certain deeds evidence of true belief rather than mere intellectual acknowledgment.

حُدُودُ (hududu) [ح-د-د]

The root ح-د-د means to limit, define boundaries, or demarcate. The word حُدُودُ (hudud) refers to Allah’s boundaries or limits - the parameters He has set. These boundaries are invisible and require internal conviction to observe, as no external force necessarily prevents their violation in private.

الْكَافِرِينَ (al-kafirin) [ك-ف-ر]

The root ك-ف-ر means to cover, conceal, or deny. The word كَافِرِينَ (kafirin) typically means disbelievers, but also carries the meaning of those who are ungrateful and who reject Allah’s authority. In this context, it refers not just to external disbelief but to internal rejection of Allah’s commands - those who knowingly disregard Allah’s boundaries despite claiming faith outwardly.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 3

The phrase يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا presents a unique grammatical construction where the preposition لِ (li) creates interpretive ambiguity. Conventionally, one might expect مِمَّا قَالُوا (mimma qalu) - “from what they said” - but the use of لِ allows for multiple valid readings. This is called الْمُشْتَرَك اللَّفْظِيّ (shared terminology) where one expression carries multiple intended meanings simultaneously. The construction can mean: (1) they return to (restore) what they originally intended by their marriage, (2) they feel remorse because of what they said, or (3) they want to go back from (undo) what they said.

The word مَوْعِظَة (maw’iza - counsel) rather than أَمْر (command) represents a deliberate rhetorical choice. This is an example of التَّلْطِيف (softening) in Divine speech, where Allah frames an obligation as counsel to emphasize its wisdom and benefit rather than mere authority. The passive voice تُوعَظُونَ (you are being counseled) also creates distance, making the instruction less direct and more contemplative.

The phrase مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَاسَّا (min qabli an yatamassa - before they touch each other) employs كِنَايَة (euphemism) - indirect reference to an explicit matter. This rhetorical device maintains dignity and modesty while conveying necessary legal information. The Quran consistently uses euphemistic language for intimate matters, setting a standard for dignified discourse.

The structure ذَٰلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ (that is what you are counseled with) uses demonstrative emphasis with ذَٰلِكُمْ followed by a passive construction, creating a sense of importance and weightiness. The بِ in بِهِ emphasizes instrumentality - this is the very means by which wisdom is conveyed.

The closing statement وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ employs الْجُمْلَة الاسْمِيَّة (nominal sentence) structure for emphasis and permanence. The present tense verb تَعْمَلُونَ (you are doing) indicates continuous awareness, and the placement of خَبِيرٌ at the end creates تَأْخِير للاهْتِمَام (delayed emphasis) - saving the most important descriptor for last.

Ayah 4

The conditional structure فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ (whoever does not find) followed by فَصِيَامُ (then fasting of) uses الْجَزَاء الْمُقَدَّم (fronted consequence) where the consequence is mentioned before completing the full conditional clause. This creates urgency and emphasizes the alternative solution.

The specification مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ (consecutive) is a crucial legal qualifier that transforms the nature of the fast. Without this specification, one might distribute the fasts across many months, but the consecutive requirement creates sustained spiritual discipline. This is an example of القَيْد المُخَصِّص (restrictive qualifier) that fundamentally alters the ruling’s application.

The phrase ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (that is so that you may believe in Allah and His Messenger) employs لام التَّعْلِيل (lam of reasoning/purpose), connecting the expiation directly to faith. The present tense تُؤْمِنُوا rather than past tense creates an ongoing relationship between action and belief - suggesting these acts continuously generate and strengthen faith.

The closing phrase وَلِلْكَافِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ presents a stark contrast through الْمُقَابَلَة (antithesis). After speaking to believers about demonstrating faith, Allah immediately contrasts their state with that of disbelievers who face painful punishment. The indefinite عَذَابٌ (a punishment) creates تَنْكِير للتَّهْوِيل (indefiniteness for magnification) - an unspecified painful punishment that the imagination fills with dread.

The parallel structure of تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ (those are Allah’s boundaries) uses the distant demonstrative تِلْكَ rather than هَذِهِ to create a sense of majesty and elevation - these are not ordinary human rules but divinely established boundaries.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 3

The practice of zihar was an ancient Arabian custom predating Islam, deeply embedded in Jahiliyyah society for potentially thousands of years. When a man declared his wife to be “like my mother’s back” (ظَهْر أُمِّي - zahru ummi), it rendered the marriage effectively over without the possibility of reconciliation or remarriage. The woman would be trapped in limbo - neither married nor divorced, unable to remarry, yet receiving no marital rights or support. This practice reflects the broader pattern in pre-Islamic Arabia where women had minimal legal protections and men wielded absolute authority over marital status.

The incident that prompted these revelations involved Khawla bint Tha’laba (RA), whose husband Aws ibn As-Samit (RA) declared zihar against her in a moment of anger. When she came to the Prophet ﷺ seeking resolution, he initially responded according to the established custom: “You have become haram for him.” This response reflected the prevailing understanding before revelation provided a new solution. Khawla (RA) persisted, arguing with the Prophet ﷺ and appealing to Allah’s justice, while Allah was hearing her complaint from above the seven heavens. This incident demonstrates the remarkable accessibility of the Prophet ﷺ and how Divine revelation responded to the real struggles of believers.

The concept of freeing slaves as expiation has precedent in Islamic law established in earlier revelations. When the Qibla changed from Jerusalem to Makkah (Surah Al-Baqarah), Allah addressed concerns about previous prayers by calling them acts of faith (iman) rather than merely ritual actions. Similarly, throughout Islamic legislation, freeing slaves represents one of the highest forms of charity and carries spiritual weight equivalent to saving a life.

The requirement of 60 consecutive days of fasting parallels the expiation mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah for those who deliberately break the Ramadan fast through intimacy. This consistency in Islamic legal principles shows how Allah’s law maintains internal coherence across different contexts.

No direct Biblical parallels exist for the specific practice of zihar, though the Torah and Gospel contain various regulations about divorce and separation. The Islamic approach of providing a path to reconciliation rather than permanent separation represents a mercy not found in the pre-Islamic Arabian system.

Coherence

Ayah 3-4

These ayahs conclude the opening narrative of Surah Al-Mujadila that began with Allah hearing the complaint of the woman who argued about her husband. The surah’s name itself - Al-Mujadila (The Pleading Woman) - centers on this incident, making these legal prescriptions the Divine response to her plea.

The transition from Ayah 2 to Ayah 3 moves from declaration (“they are not their mothers”) to consequence (“those who do this, then want to return…”). This represents a shift from theological correction to practical legislation - Allah first establishes truth, then provides the method to rectify violation of that truth.

The phrase ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ (“that is so you may believe in Allah and His Messenger”) creates a profound connection within the surah. The woman came arguing with (تُجَادِلُكَ) the Messenger, and Allah was hearing (يَسْمَعُ) from above. Now Allah reveals that following these commands demonstrates faith in both Allah and His Messenger - validating the woman’s appeal to both Divine and Prophetic authority.

The remainder of Surah Al-Mujadila (Ayahs 5-22) shifts to discussing hypocrites who hold secret councils and conspire against the Prophet ﷺ and the Muslim community. The coherence between these seemingly disparate topics lies in the theme of private versus public faith. The opening incident involves private marital issues brought into the open for resolution, demonstrating genuine faith that seeks Allah’s guidance. In contrast, the hypocrites appear righteous publicly while plotting secretly, demonstrating false faith that conceals disbelief.

This surah connects to Surah Al-Hadid (57) immediately before it, which discusses the distinction between outer appearance and inner reality of faith. Al-Hadid describes Judgment Day when some believers will have light radiating from them while others claiming belief will be locked behind walls, separated from the truly faithful. Both surahs explore the two dimensions of faith - external profession and internal reality.

The connection extends to Surah At-Tahrim (66), several surahs later in the same Madani cluster (Surahs 57-66). At-Tahrim uniquely addresses believers using the phrase يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (O you who disbelieve) when speaking about Judgment Day - indicating that some who appear as believers in this life will be addressed as disbelievers in the Hereafter when hearts are judged rather than external appearances.

The placement of these family-related legal rulings at the opening of a surah primarily concerned with political hypocrisy and community betrayal teaches a fundamental Islamic principle: the integrity of public life depends on the integrity of private life. Corruption in intimate relationships leads to corruption in social and political relationships. The same principles of fearing Allah apply in one’s home as in one’s governance.

Psychological, Political & Social

Ayah 3

The requirement to free a slave before resuming marital relations creates a powerful psychological connection between the harm done to one’s wife and the harm of enslavement. Allah structures the expiation so the perpetrator must relieve someone else’s suffering to make up for the suffering he caused his wife. This teaches empathy through action - you trapped her in an impossible situation of being neither married nor divorced, so now free someone trapped in slavery. The correlation is deliberate: you want her freedom restored, so restore someone else’s freedom first.

The phrase “before they touch each other” establishes a mandatory waiting period that serves multiple psychological purposes. For someone who may have declared zihar in a moment of anger and later wanted to resume intimacy that very day, being forced to wait creates space for reflection. In our modern age of instant gratification, this mandated pause forces contemplation about the weight of one’s words and the consequences of emotional outbursts.

The 60 consecutive days of fasting represents a sustained discipline that cannot be gamed or shortcut. This extended period serves as behavioral conditioning - during Ramadan, intimacy is permitted during nights, but these 60 fasts prohibit marital relations entirely throughout their duration. For someone who “couldn’t wait three hours,” being required to wait 60 days teaches profound lessons about self-control and the seriousness of one’s declarations. The consecutive requirement means that any slip resets the entire process, ensuring genuine commitment rather than mere performance.

The use of the word مَوْعِظَة (counsel) rather than command reveals psychological wisdom - Allah frames these requirements as advice that should affect the heart, not merely external compliance. This recognizes that true change comes from internal conviction, not just fear of punishment. When someone understands why they must do something, compliance becomes internalized rather than resentful obligation.

The statement “Allah is Aware of your experience” (وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ) addresses the universal human tendency to justify wrongdoing through context: “You don’t know what she said to me,” or “You don’t know what I was going through.” Allah preempts this by declaring comprehensive awareness of all circumstances - yet still requiring the expiation. This teaches that extenuating circumstances may explain behavior but don’t eliminate accountability.

Ayah 4

The three-tiered system of expiation (free a slave, fast 60 days, feed 60 people) demonstrates social awareness and economic flexibility. Allah recognizes that not everyone has equal means, so He provides alternatives that maintain the principle while acknowledging varying capabilities. This structure appears throughout Islamic law - obligations scaled to ability, ensuring no one is excluded from the possibility of redemption.

The connection between fasting and feeding others reveals the social consciousness embedded in Islamic jurisprudence. When someone cannot fast due to permanent inability (old age, chronic illness), they must feed others instead. This transforms personal limitation into social benefit - your inability to fast becomes someone else’s meal. The community’s vulnerable members receive support through the religious obligations of others.

The phrase “that is so you may believe in Allah and His Messenger” transforms legal compliance into faith demonstration. This addresses a profound psychological reality: faith is not merely internal conviction but manifests through action. Someone claiming faith while disregarding Allah’s commands reveals dissonance between profession and reality. By making these actions evidence of faith, Allah establishes an objective standard - your deeds testify to your belief more reliably than your words.

The warning about “painful punishment for disbelievers” (وَلِلْكَافِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ) introduces psychological tension - are these “disbelievers” only those who openly reject Islam, or do they include those who claim faith but habitually violate Allah’s boundaries? The context suggests the latter, creating healthy fear that profession of faith means nothing without corresponding action. This guards against the dangerous self-deception where people assume external religious identity guarantees salvation.

Social Implications

The legislation around zihar represents revolutionary social reform. Pre-Islamic Arabia allowed men to indefinitely suspend marriages while preventing women from remarrying - a form of abandonment without legal resolution. By providing a path to reconciliation with clear conditions, Allah gave women legal standing and protection previously denied to them.

The requirement to free a slave as primary expiation elevated the status of enslaved people in Arabian society. Making their liberation equivalent to resolving a marital grievance dignified their humanity in a society that treated them as property. This is one of many Quranic mechanisms gradually dismantling slavery from within - making freedom a form of worship and religious obligation.

The public nature of these revelations - where a private marital dispute became subject of Divine legislation - established the principle that Islam protects individuals even in their most private relationships. A woman could appeal to Allah and His Messenger against injustice in her home, and Allah would respond. This stands in stark contrast to modern contexts where domestic issues remain hidden and victims have no voice.

Political Dimensions

The surah’s structure - family law followed by political treachery - teaches that governance integrity depends on household integrity. Societies cannot eliminate political corruption through external mechanisms (audits, investigations, arrests) if the foundational units of society lack internal accountability to Allah. The family becomes the training ground for the principles that must govern public life: truthfulness, accountability, boundaries, and consequences.

The hypocrites discussed later in the surah held secret councils to undermine the Prophet’s ﷺ leadership while appearing loyal publicly. In contrast, this opening incident shows believers bringing their private struggles into the open for Allah’s resolution. The political lesson: genuine faith creates transparency and seeks Allah’s judgment, while false faith creates duplicity and hidden agendas.

The phrase تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللَّهِ (those are Allah’s boundaries) establishes that Divine law governs both private and public spheres. The boundaries are “invisible” - no police enforce them in bedrooms - yet they remain binding. This creates citizens who self-regulate based on internal conviction rather than external surveillance, the foundation of any functional society.

Quranic Wisdom

Ayah 3-4

The most profound wisdom in these ayahs lies in their treatment of words as having real consequences. In modern culture, we often dismiss hurtful speech with “they’re just words” or “I didn’t mean it.” Allah teaches that words carry weight - they can trap people, hurt them deeply, and create obligations that require significant effort to resolve. The fact that a verbal declaration triggers such serious expiation demonstrates that Allah takes our speech seriously even when we don’t.

The requirement to free a slave before reconciliation teaches that mercy must flow outward. You want mercy for your situation? Show mercy to someone else first. You want your relationship freed from this impossible circumstance? Free someone else from their impossible circumstance. This principle - that receiving blessing requires giving blessing - appears throughout the Quran and represents a fundamental spiritual law.

The three options for expiation (freeing a slave, fasting 60 days, feeding 60 people) reveal that redemption is always possible regardless of circumstances. Allah doesn’t make repentance impossible or forgiveness unattainable. There’s always a path forward, always a way to make things right, though it requires genuine effort and sacrifice. This reflects Allah’s mercy - He wants reconciliation, not permanent rupture.

The phrase “before they touch each other” teaches the spiritual discipline of delayed gratification in service of a higher principle. In an age of instant everything - instant communication, instant entertainment, instant intimacy through pornography - Allah mandates a pause. This waiting period forces confrontation with one’s actions and creates space for genuine transformation rather than quick fixes that leave patterns unchanged.

The 60 consecutive days represent the principle that spiritual growth requires sustained effort, not sporadic attempts. Missing one day resets everything because transformation comes through consistent discipline, not occasional good intentions. This applies beyond fasting - real change in any area requires sustained, focused effort over time.

The use of موعظة (counsel that affects the heart) rather than أمر (command) teaches that Allah’s laws are not arbitrary impositions but wisdom designed for our benefit. When we approach Divine legislation with our hearts, not just our bodies, we discover the wisdom embedded in every ruling. The goal isn’t mere compliance but understanding and internalization.

The statement “Allah is Aware of your experience” provides profound comfort and warning simultaneously. Comfort because Allah truly understands context, circumstances, pressures, and struggles - He’s not a distant judge lacking information. Warning because all justifications and excuses are already known and factored in, yet the obligation remains. This teaches that Allah’s awareness of our struggles makes His commands more meaningful, not less binding.

The connection between these family-focused ayahs and the political content that follows teaches that personal integrity precedes social reform. We cannot build righteous societies while neglecting our households. We cannot eliminate corruption in politics while accepting it in our homes. The ummah’s restoration begins not with grand political movements but with individuals being mindful of Allah in their most private moments.

The phrase “that is so you may believe in Allah and His Messenger” reveals that actions don’t just express faith - they create and strengthen it. Fasting for 60 days, feeding 60 people, or freeing a slave will change you. The act itself generates and deepens belief. This teaches that sometimes we don’t wait to feel faithful before acting; we act in order to become faithful. Faith grows through obedience, not just before it.

The warning about “painful punishment for disbelievers” forces honest self-examination. Are we among those who truly believe, or do we merely carry the label while our hearts remain unmoved by Allah’s commands? Judgment Day will not recognize cultural Islam, inherited Islam, or identity Islam - only the Islam that lived in the heart and manifested in deeds. This section of the surah calls us to examine whether our faith is genuine or merely nominal, whether we truly submit to Allah’s boundaries or just perform religious identity while living according to our desires.

Ultimately, these ayahs teach that Allah’s laws are acts of divine love. Every boundary Allah sets, every obligation He imposes, every expiation He requires serves to protect us, purify us, and bring us closer to Him. The woman who came pleading received not just a legal ruling but comprehensive care - Allah heard her pain, dignified her struggle, corrected the injustice, and provided a path forward. This is the nature of Divine legislation: it heals, guides, and elevates those who submit to it with sincere hearts.

Ayah 9–11 · linked video: 07. Al-Mujadila (Ayah 09-11)

Root Word Analysis

The root word نَجْوَى (najwa) comes from the three-letter root ن-ج-و (n-j-w), which fundamentally means “to separate” or “to set apart.” In classical Arabic, najwa originally referred to the elevated edges of a valley—the high peaks that separate themselves from the valley floor. The core semantic meaning revolves around separation and distinction.

From this same root, we derive several related terms: نَجَا (naja) meaning “to rescue” or “to escape,” which carries the sense of being separated from danger or harm; نَجَاة (najat) meaning “salvation” or “deliverance”; and مُنَاجَاة (munajat) meaning “intimate conversation with Allah” in prayer, which involves separating oneself for private worship.

The word najwa specifically denotes a private council or secret meeting where certain individuals separate themselves from a larger group to discuss matters away from others who might be relevant to the conversation. The linguistic structure emphasizes the act of deliberate separation—not merely a private gathering, but one where relevant parties are intentionally excluded.

The morphological form suggests an ongoing action or state, indicating that najwa is not a one-time occurrence but can become a pattern of behavior. When Allah uses this term in the Quran, He is highlighting the nature of separation that occurs when believers fragment into exclusive groups rather than maintaining unity in their consultations.

The related term إِثْم (ithm - sin) appears alongside najwa in these verses. Ithm derives from the root أ-ث-م and denotes wrongdoing or transgression. When combined with najwa, it describes secret meetings characterized by sinful intent.

عُدْوَان (udwan - aggression/animosity) comes from the root ع-د-و, meaning “to transgress” or “to be hostile.” This term adds another dimension to prohibited najwa—meetings motivated by hatred or animosity toward others.

مَعْصِيَة (ma’siyah - disobedience) derives from ع-ص-ي, meaning “to disobey” or “to rebel.” When najwa involves ma’siyat al-rasul (disobedience to the Messenger صلى الله عليه وسلم), it becomes a religious transgression.

The imperative verb تَفَسَّحُوا (tafassahu) from the root ف-س-ح means “to make room” or “to create space.” The form II intensive verb emphasizes the need for active, generous accommodation. Allah uses this command to establish proper etiquette in Muslim gatherings, contrasting with the exclusionary nature of najwa.

انشُزُوا (unshuzu) from the root ن-ش-ز means “to rise up” or “to get up quickly.” The morphological form suggests immediate, energetic action without delay or hesitation.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

The passage employs the vocative يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (Ya ayyuha alladhina amanu - “O you who have believed”) multiple times, creating a direct address that commands attention and establishes the audience as believers with responsibilities. This rhetorical device personalizes the message and creates accountability.

The conditional structure إِذَا تَنَاجَيْتُمْ (idha tanajaitum - “when you hold secret counsel”) uses the particle إِذَا (idha) which indicates an expected, recurring event rather than a hypothetical situation. This grammatical choice suggests that secret meetings are an inevitable part of community life, making the guidance practical rather than theoretical.

Allah employs emphatic negation with فَلَا تَتَنَاجَوْا (fa la tatanajaw - “then do not hold secret counsel”). The particle فَ (fa) creates immediate consequence, while the prohibition لَا (la) with the imperfect verb emphasizes ongoing prohibition—“do not continue” or “do not persist” in such meetings.

The trilogy of prohibited motives—بِالْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ الرَّسُولِ (bil-ithmi wal-udwani wa ma’siyati al-rasul) uses the conjunctive وَ (wa) to link three escalating categories: sin, animosity and disobedience to the Messenger صلى الله عليه وسلم. The grammatical structure moves from general wrongdoing to specific religious transgression.

The contrasting command وَتَنَاجَوْا بِالْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوَىٰ (wa tanajaw bil-birri wal-taqwa - “but hold secret counsel in righteousness and taqwa”) uses the same verb form but with positive prepositions, creating rhetorical balance—if you must meet privately, let it be characterized by these qualities.

The phrase إِنَّمَا النَّجْوَىٰ مِنَ الشَّيْطَانِ (innama al-najwa min al-shaitan) employs the particle إِنَّمَا (innama) which creates exclusive restriction—“secret counsel is ONLY from Satan.” This is an exceptionally strong rhetorical device that establishes the default nature of najwa as satanic unless actively purified.

The purposive clause لِيَحْزُنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (liyahzuna alladhina amanu - “to grieve those who have believed”) uses the lam of purpose (لِ) to reveal Satan’s strategic objective. The grammatical structure exposes the hidden motive behind seemingly innocent private meetings.

The exceptive phrase وَلَيْسَ بِضَارِّهِمْ شَيْئًا إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ (wa laysa bidarrihim shay’an illa bi-idhni Allah - “but he will not harm them at all except by Allah’s permission”) employs إِلَّا (illa) for exception, establishing divine sovereignty over all outcomes. The absolute negation شَيْئًا (shay’an - “anything at all”) emphasizes total divine control.

In the command تَفَسَّحُوا فِي الْمَجَالِسِ فَافْسَحُوا (tafassahu fil-majalisi fafsahu), the repetition of the root ف-س-ح creates emphasis through variation—the intensive Form V followed by the simple Form I. The particle فَ (fa) creates immediate consequence: “when told to make room, THEN make room.”

The reciprocal promise يَفْسَحِ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ (yafsahi Allahu lakum - “Allah will make room for you”) uses the same verbal root but with Allah as the subject, creating beautiful rhetorical symmetry—as you make space, Allah makes space for you. This is divine reciprocity expressed through grammatical parallelism.

The phrase وَإِذَا قِيلَ انشُزُوا فَانشُزُوا (wa idha qila unshuzu fanshuzu) employs the passive voice قِيلَ (qila - “it is said”) to indicate that the command comes from legitimate authority. The immediate response marker فَ (fa) again emphasizes swift obedience.

The concluding statement يَرْفَعِ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجَاتٍ (yarfa’i Allahu alladhina amanu minkum walladhina utu al-’ilma darajat) uses the accusative درجات (darajat - “ranks”) as a specification of manner—Allah raises them BY ranks or IN ranks. The passive أُوتُوا (utu - “were given”) for knowledge emphasizes that true knowledge is a divine gift, not merely acquired.

The final phrase وَاللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (wallahu bima ta’maluna khabir - “Allah is fully aware of what you do”) employs the intensive form خَبِير (khabir) which denotes expert, complete awareness. The present tense تَعْمَلُونَ (ta’maluna) indicates ongoing actions, reminding believers of constant divine observation.

Historical & Biblical Context

These verses were revealed in Madinah during a period when the Muslim community faced internal challenges from munafiqun (hypocrites) who had outwardly accepted Islam but worked to undermine community unity. Unlike in Makkah where opposition was external and clear, in Madinah the threat became more insidious—enemies within who appeared as friends.

The historical context includes multiple incidents. One significant occasion occurred during the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم Friday sermon when honored companions who had participated in the Battle of Badr arrived late and stood at the back of the mosque. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم commanded those seated in front to make room for these veterans. This created a teaching moment about honor, sacrifice and the hierarchy of merit in Islam—not based on arrival time but on contribution and sacrifice for the faith.

The hypocrites seized upon this incident to sow discord, claiming the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم was showing favoritism and treating early arrivals unfairly. They positioned themselves as defenders of “social justice” while actually undermining the principle that those who sacrifice more deserve greater honor. This reveals their fundamental misunderstanding—or deliberate distortion—of Islamic values where recognition follows contribution, not the reverse.

The practice of najwa had precedent in both Arab culture and earlier religious communities. In pre-Islamic Arabia, tribal councils would often conduct secret meetings to plot against rivals. The verses warn believers against adopting these jahiliyyah (ignorance-era) practices within the Islamic community.

Regarding Biblical parallels, the concept of secret councils plotting against the righteous appears throughout scripture. In the Old Testament, conspirators would gather secretly against prophets and the faithful. The Book of Psalms repeatedly mentions enemies who “take crafty counsel” and “consult together” in secret (Psalm 83:3). Similarly, the plot against Prophet Isa (AS) involved secret meetings of religious authorities who conspired to arrest and execute him.

The theme of Iblis (Satan) refusing to prostrate to Adam (AS) provides the theological foundation for understanding najwa as satanic. When Allah جل جلاله commanded the angels to prostrate to Adam (AS), Iblis was present and heard the command but remained silent, harboring his objection privately. Unlike the angels who openly questioned Allah’s wisdom about creating humans—an inquiry made in genuine seeking of understanding—Iblis kept his arrogance hidden until the moment of disobedience. This established the pattern: secret harboring of opposition rather than honest, open inquiry represents satanic behavior.

The angels’ questioning of Adam’s (AS) creation demonstrates the Islamic principle of legitimate inquiry. When they asked “Will You place therein one who causes corruption and sheds blood?” (2:30), they were not being disobedient but seeking understanding. Allah جل جلاله answered their question, and they accepted the wisdom. This contrasts sharply with Iblis’s secret resentment that he never voiced until his refusal to prostrate became public.

Historically, the verses about making room in gatherings also connect to the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم practice of inclusivity. When Christian delegations from Najran visited Madinah, they were hosted in the Prophet’s mosque. They even conducted their own worship there. This exemplified the Islamic principle that houses of Allah should be open and welcoming to all who seek truth, not exclusive clubs for members only.

The immediate post-revelation period saw the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم implementing these principles strictly. After these verses were revealed, whenever meetings concluded, companions would disperse immediately rather than lingering in small groups. This prevented the formation of cliques and the perception of secret dealings that could erode community trust.

The practice of dispersing immediately after gatherings also had practical security implications. In Madinah, with threats from within and without, preventing the formation of conspiratorial groups was essential for community safety. The hypocrites could not organize their schemes if they were unable to gather privately after general meetings.

From a prophetic narrative perspective, these verses continue themes from earlier prophets. Prophet Musa (AS) faced similar challenges with conspirators among his own people—the incident of Korah (Qarun) who gathered supporters to undermine Musa’s (AS) leadership. Prophet Nuh (AS) dealt with secret mockery and plotting among his people who would meet privately to ridicule his message.

The revelation of these verses in Surah Al-Mujadila is particularly appropriate given the surah’s opening—a woman (Khawlah bint Tha’labah رضي الله عنها) who was not afraid to openly question a ruling, bringing her concern directly to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم rather than complaining privately to others. She exemplifies the opposite of najwa: transparent, direct communication with proper authority rather than secretive undermining.

Coherence

Surah Al-Mujadila maintains remarkable thematic unity centered on proper communication, social dynamics and the contrast between believers and hypocrites. The surah opens with a woman who has a legitimate grievance and takes it directly to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم—a model of transparent, appropriate communication. Allah جل جلاله honors her by saying “Indeed Allah has heard the speech of she who argues with you concerning her husband” (58:1). This establishes the standard: open, direct communication with proper authority.

The verses on najwa (9-11) form a cohesive unit addressing the opposite problem: secretive, divisive communication that bypasses proper channels and undermines community unity. The connection is clear—just as Khawlah (RA) demonstrated proper communication, these verses warn against improper communication through secret councils.

Within this three-verse unit, the coherence is carefully structured:

Verse 9 establishes the problem: secret meetings characterized by sin, animosity and disobedience. It provides the antidote: if you must meet privately, let it be characterized by righteousness and taqwa. The verse concludes with the command to have taqwa of Allah “to Whom you will be gathered,” connecting private actions with ultimate accountability.

Verse 10 reveals the source: “Secret counsel is only from Satan.” This connects to the surah’s broader theme of distinguishing believers from hypocrites. Satan’s objective is explained—“to grieve those who have believed”—which links to the community impact of divisive behavior. The verse concludes with divine sovereignty: Satan can only harm by Allah’s permission, so believers should trust in Allah completely.

Verse 11 provides practical solutions: proper gathering etiquette that prevents najwa. When told to make room, make room—this creates inclusion rather than exclusion. When told to disperse, disperse immediately—this prevents the formation of splinter groups. The promise follows: “Allah will make room for you” and “Allah will raise those among you who believe and those who were given knowledge, by degrees.”

The logical flow moves from problem (najwa’s nature) to root cause (Satan) to solution (proper gathering protocol), creating a complete therapeutic approach.

These verses connect to earlier passages in the surah. Verses 7-8 discuss Allah’s complete knowledge of secret conversations, establishing divine awareness of all private dealings. This omniscience makes najwa futile—Allah knows what they conceal. Verses 9-11 then provide guidance on how to conduct gatherings properly given this divine knowledge.

The connection to subsequent verses (12-13) is also clear. After establishing proper gathering etiquette and warning against secret councils, Allah introduces the practice of giving charity before private consultation with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم. This creates a litmus test—those who genuinely need to consult privately will pay the charity, while those merely seeking influence or engaging in politics will be discouraged. This continues the theme of purifying intentions in private dealings.

Thematically, these verses connect to the broader Quranic discourse on community unity. The command to make room in gatherings recalls the verses about prayer formation where believers stand in organized ranks (61:4). Just as physical ranks must be straight and unified in prayer, social ranks in gatherings must be open and accommodating.

The promise “Allah will make room for you” connects to the Quranic principle of divine reciprocity found throughout the Quran: “Remember Me, I will remember you” (2:152); “If you are grateful, I will surely increase you” (14:7). Here the reciprocity is spatial and social—as you create openness, Allah creates openness for you in provision, guidance and blessings.

The phrase “Allah will raise those among you who believe and those who were given knowledge, by degrees” connects to verses throughout the Quran emphasizing the elevated status of knowledge. “Say, ‘Are those who know equal to those who do not know?’” (39:9). The coherence here shows that true elevation comes from Allah based on faith and knowledge, not from political maneuvering in secret meetings.

The warning against disobedience to the Messenger (ma’siyat al-rasul) in these verses connects to the surah’s opening where Allah speaks of proper channels of authority and the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وسلم role as the final arbiter of disputes. Khawlah (RA) brought her case to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وسلم; she didn’t gather supporters to pressure him or undermine his authority.

The reference to making room for those who arrived late after having participated in Badr connects to the Quranic theme that merit in Islam is based on sacrifice and contribution, not seniority or political skill. This recalls “Not equal among you are those who spent and fought before the conquest” (57:10) and “Allah has preferred in rank those who fought with their properties and their lives over those who sat” (4:95).

The immediate dispersal after meetings prevents the post-meeting meetings that would constitute najwa, creating practical coherence between the prohibition and its solution. You cannot have secret breakaway groups if everyone disperses immediately when gatherings conclude.

The final phrase “Allah is fully aware of what you do” creates closure by returning to the theme of divine omniscience that runs throughout the surah. Just as Allah heard Khawlah’s complaint (verse 1) and Allah knows what they conceal (verse 7), here Allah remains fully aware even as people rise and disperse. This divine awareness brackets the entire discussion of social dynamics.

Psychological, Political & Social

These verses provide deep psychological insights into group dynamics, power structures and human behavior in organizational settings. The phenomenon of najwa reveals several psychological realities:

The need for recognition drives much destructive behavior. When people feel their contributions are not acknowledged in larger settings, they seek smaller venues where they can feel important. This is the psychology of cliques and factions. The hypocrites in Madinah exemplified this—unable to achieve genuine status through sacrifice and contribution, they sought influence through political maneuvering and private alliances.

Psychologically, secret meetings create an in-group/out-group dynamic that is inherently divisive. Those included in the najwa feel special, privileged, part of the “real” decision-making. Those excluded feel marginalized, suspicious and resentful. This destroys the trust essential for healthy communities. Social psychology research confirms that perceived unfairness and exclusion are among the most potent destroyers of group cohesion.

The verses reveal that Satan’s strategy is psychological warfare—“to grieve those who have believed.” This shows profound understanding of how division and suspicion demoralize communities. When believers see others meeting privately, speculation begins: What are they discussing? Are they plotting against me? Do they dislike me? This anxiety and suspicion drain energy and enthusiasm, which is exactly Satan’s objective.

The linking of najwa with sin, animosity and disobedience reveals the psychological progression: Secret meetings begin with disagreement or dislike (animosity), proceed to discussions that violate ethical principles (sin) and culminate in undermining legitimate authority (disobedience to the Messenger صلى الله عليه وسلم). This is a recognizable pattern in organizational dysfunction.

The command to make room when others arrive addresses the psychology of territoriality and status. Humans naturally claim space and resist accommodating newcomers, especially when comfortable. The Islamic solution is counterintuitive—actively create space for others, especially those who have earned honor through sacrifice. This trains believers to overcome natural selfishness and status-seeking.

The instruction to disperse immediately after meetings addresses the phenomenon of “meetings after the meeting” where the real decisions are made. In many organizations, formal meetings are merely theater while actual decisions occur in informal gatherings afterward. Islam prohibits this by requiring immediate dispersal, ensuring that decisions are made transparently in proper forums.

Politically, these verses establish crucial principles for Islamic governance and organizational structure:

Leadership decisions should be made transparently in appropriate councils, not through backroom dealings. The requirement to conduct najwa only with righteousness and taqwa means that even necessary private consultations must serve the common good, not factional interests.

The principle that merit-based hierarchy is legitimate—making room for those who sacrificed more—acknowledges that not all opinions carry equal weight. Democracy does not mean all opinions are equally valuable; those who have contributed more, sacrificed more and demonstrated better judgment earn greater consideration. This is just and functional.

The verses implicitly recognize that not all gatherings can be completely open—hence permission for najwa characterized by righteousness and taqwa. Military strategy, sensitive personal matters and certain administrative decisions require privacy. The key is motive and content, not privacy itself.

The warning that Satan’s najwa aims “to grieve those who have believed” but “he will not harm them at all except by Allah’s permission” teaches crucial political wisdom: conspiracies feel threatening, but ultimate control rests with Allah. Believers should be vigilant against divisive tactics while maintaining confident trust in divine protection.

Socially, these verses address the disease of factionalism that destroys communities. Muslims worldwide struggle with ethnic divisions, sectarian splits and political factions within mosques and organizations. The verses provide the antidote:

Create inclusive spaces—“make room” means welcoming diverse people, not just those who look like us, think like us or worship exactly as we do. The modern Muslim community’s tendency to form ethnic enclaves and sect-specific institutions directly violates this principle.

Prioritize unity over comfort—making room may require personal discomfort, squeezing together, giving up preferred spots. The greater good of community cohesion outweighs individual convenience.

Disperse rather than clique—the command to leave immediately after gatherings prevents the formation of social cliques that exclude others and breed suspicion. In contemporary contexts, this applies to WhatsApp groups, private email chains and social media circles that exclude parts of the community.

The sociological insight about meetings reveals that the problem is not privacy per se but exclusion of relevant parties. A meeting becomes najwa when people who should be involved are deliberately kept out. This distinguishes legitimate private consultations from divisive secret councils.

The verse’s application to family dynamics is particularly relevant. In households, some members may meet privately to discuss others, forming alliances against family members. This destroys family trust and unity. The Islamic principle is: if it concerns someone, involve them; if it is not your business, do not discuss it.
